source_display: AI as Brain Crutch for ADHD
evidence: Vrijen shared his personal interest in building AI tools as 'brain crutches' for ADHD—filling gaps between therapy, medication, and coaching
Vrijen warned against the 'death by a thousand cuts' of endless unpaid pilots. Instead, seek paid pilots with scoped changes and identify design partners willing to work with the product as-is
Vrijen defined product-market fit using the Supreme Court's obscenity standard: 'you know it when you see it'—when you have it, you don't think about it; when you don't, you obsess over it constantly

Chris expressed concern about organizational sales conversations dangling large contracts in exchange for custom feature development. Vrijen warned against the 'chicken or egg' problem and the risk of over-optimizing for a single stakeholder at the expense of broader product fit
Chris emphasized the advantage of building for his own community—having ADHD himself and being a former teacher/coach provides validation and trust with users
Chris revealed he's been building his edtech product for almost a year, leveraging AI despite being a former special education teacher rather than a developer
Chris revealed having 65,000 active email subscribers built over years—a significant distribution asset
The core concept is a 'marketplace of all possible futures' - allowing users to 'jump into' career simulations mapped to real jobs and companies

Users work with three synthetic co-workers (engineer, data analyst, UX researcher) who have different priorities and responsibilities. The collaboration leads to feature building that gets stored in a portfolio
Max used the metaphor of a 'neopet' - anytime you train or deploy your AI requires 'energy,' which is where they monetize through usage-based pricing

Maximilian emphasized that his product creates portfolios first and assessments second, acknowledging that assessments are difficult to execute effectively. He criticized tools like HackerRank for not grounding results in actual business value

Maximilian observed that engineers more than one step removed from the product were most vulnerable. Those accepting tickets from queues without touching product understanding were let go, while those with ownership of product parts remained

The conversation critiqued abstracted roles like product owner and project manager, suggesting these roles exist primarily to organize attention but don't add direct value to products. Maximilian argued these roles are 'done' in the current AI-enabled landscape

target_display: Hiring Signal Market Burned by Snake Oil
evidence: Vrijen observed that the market for novel hiring signals is small because snake oil assessment companies have burned goodwill in the past. However, he noted that large language models enable processing more signal, suggesting increasing openness to alternative evaluation methods
Maximilian shared that he's preparing to launch his new product in approximately one month
Vrijen introduced context engineering as the foundational practice: not prompting the AI directly, but cultivating the context it uses to generate desired outputs. He outlined three strategic levels
The session focused on moving beyond individual AI techniques to building a coherent personal AI operating system through context engineering at three levels: conversation, environment, and pipeline
Ben: 'you can unhobble your AI a lot when you give it access to your files and data... when you give the AI its own home'
Logan: 'job security is dead and you need to be building career security for yourself... it has a lot to do with being findable and being visible'
Logan: 'come up with like two to three things that would really delight me... what's LinkedIn sitting on that they should be showing us'
Vrijen argued the core issue isn't a technology or tactics problem but a mental model problem—users collect techniques without understanding how they integrate into effective systems
Ben Guo (co-founder of Zo Computer and Vrijen as a power Zo user)
Ben: 'I was traveling to San Francisco yesterday and I was just doing this while I was waiting in line at the airport' - exploiting AI integration with mobile channels to work in passive moments
